input_candidates.truth_value: The volume fraction of small angle grain boundaries increased greatly during deformation, exceeding 51% at true strain 0.27 for H90 (from 26% at 0.03, 31% at 0.10, 45% at 0.20).
input_candidates.prediction_value: The steel exhibited three-stage work hardening characteristics during tensile test: stage 1 with rapidly decreasing work hardening rate associated with ferrite deformation; stage 2 linked to TRIP effect where work hardening rate increased with strain in true strain range 0.03-0.15 and then decreased gently after 0.15 due to competition between increased stress and stress relaxation; stage 3 with quickly decreasing work hardening rate associated with deformation localization of ferrite or martensite.

input_candidates.truth_value: Schmid factor distribution: at true strain 0.03, >70% of retained austenite had Schmid factors 0.45-0.48; average decreased with strain; at 0.20, >60% had Schmid factor <0.40; at 0.27, volume fraction of austenite with Schmid factor <0.34 decreased to 5%.
input_candidates.prediction_value: For H90 during tensile deformation, blocky austenite grains positioned at ferrite triple junction boundaries transformed into martensite first due to higher localized stress and triaxial stress state, while lath-like austenite grains located between ferrite laths or embedded in single ferrite grains with lower Schmid factor underwent rotations firstly.

input_candidates.truth_value: In conclusion, the best combination of mechanical properties was achieved for the steel solution treated at 900°C for 1 h, attributed to TRIP effect and high dislocation density, with UTS of 836.4 MPa, YS of 473.6 MPa, and TE of 32% (note: text mentions 846 MPa in abstract, but 836.4 MPa in conclusion; discrepancy kept as in paper).

candidates.truth_value: The volume fraction of small angle grain boundaries increased greatly during deformation, exceeding 51% at true strain 0.27 for H90 (from 26% at 0.03, 31% at 0.10, 45% at 0.20).
